curriculum_source: CFA Program 2027 Level III, Volume 5 (Ethical and Professional Standards), Learning Module 4: Guidance for Standard III: Duties to Clients

reading.reading_name: Guidance for Standard III: Duties to Clients

drills.los_text: demonstrate a thorough knowledge of the CFA Institute Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct by interpreting the Code and Standards in various situations involving issues of professional integrity
drills.reading_name: Guidance for Standard III: Duties to Clients

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 1 Stewart/Goodner*: hired to manage pension fund. Duty of loyalty, prudence, and care is owed to *most likely*:

options.A: participants and beneficiaries of Goodner's pension plan.
options.B: management of Goodner.
options.C: shareholders of Goodner.

rationales.A:
Full statement: participants and beneficiaries of Goodner's pension plan; when the manager is responsible for portfolios of pension plans or trusts, the client is not the person or entity that hires the manager but, rather, the beneficiaries of the plan or trust; duty of loyalty is owed to the ultimate beneficiaries.

Correct. Per Standard III(A) Guidance on Identifying the Client: 'When the manager is responsible for the portfolios of pension plans or trusts, however, the client is not the person or entity that hires the manager but, rather, the beneficiaries of the plan or trust. The duty of loyalty is owed to the ultimate beneficiaries.'
rationales.B: Not management.
rationales.C: Not shareholders.

questions.stem: *Example 1 Wiley/Miller*: bank pension trustee; Newton tenders for Miller (25% above market); Wiley heavily purchases Miller stock to preserve friendly management (bank does business with Miller); heavy purchases cause Newton to retract bid. *Most likely*:

options.A: no violation — beneficial for pension.
options.B: Wiley, acting on behalf of his employer which was trustee for pension plan, clearly violated Standard III(A).
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Self-interest violation.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Wiley, acting on behalf of his employer which was trustee for pension plan, clearly violated Standard III(A); he used pension plan to perpetuate existing management, perhaps to detriment of plan participants and company's shareholders, and to benefit himself; Wiley's responsibilities to plan participants and beneficiaries must take precedence over any ties of his bank to corporate managers and over his self-interest; Wiley had duty to examine takeover offer on its own merits and to make an independent decision; guiding principle is appropriateness of investment decision to the pension plan, not whether decision benefited Wiley or company that hired him.

Correct. Per Example 1 Comment: 'In this instance, Wiley, acting on behalf of his employer, which was the trustee for a pension plan, clearly violated Standard III(A). He used the pension plan to perpetuate existing management, perhaps to the detriment of plan participants and the company's shareholders, and to benefit himself. Wiley's responsibilities to the plan participants and beneficiaries must take precedence over any ties of his bank to corporate managers and over his self-interest.'
rationales.C: Beneficiaries first.

questions.stem: *Example 2 Jackson/JNI*: uses Thompson (high commission, average research/execution) for most JNI trading; Thompson absorbs JNI overhead expenses including rent. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because Thompson's research and execution quality are average rather than deficient.
options.B: Directing brokerage in exchange for overhead reimbursement is acceptable if trade prices are competitive.
options.C: Jackson is breaching his responsibilities by using client brokerage for services that do not benefit JNI clients and by not obtaining best price and best execution for JNI clients.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Client brokerage was spent on services (rent, overhead) that do not benefit JNI's clients, and best execution was not obtained — both breach the duty.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Client commissions are client assets; spending them on the manager's overhead violates loyalty regardless of price competitiveness.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Jackson is breaching his responsibilities by using client brokerage for services that do not benefit JNI clients and by not obtaining best price and best execution for JNI clients; because Jackson is not upholding his duty of loyalty, he is violating Standard III(A).

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment: 'Jackson is breaching his responsibilities by using client brokerage for services that do not benefit JNI clients and by not obtaining best price and best execution for JNI clients. Because Jackson is not upholding his duty of loyalty, he is violating Standard III(A).'

questions.stem: *Example 3 Everett/Scott*: existing client Crayton directs Everett to use Scott exclusively; Everett also directs trades to Scott from OTHER clients (without their knowledge) to induce Scott to send new business to her. *Most likely*:

options.A: Everett may direct new client trades exclusively through Scott Company as long as Everett receives best price and execution on trades or receives written statement from new clients that she is not to seek best price and execution and they are aware of consequence.
options.B: There is no violation because clients benefit when their manager maintains strong broker relationships.
options.C: Directed brokerage from Crayton was itself improper, since clients may not choose their broker.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Everett may direct new client trades exclusively through Scott Company as long as Everett receives best price and execution on trades or receives written statement from new clients that she is not to seek best price and execution and they are aware of consequence; may trade other accounts through Scott as reward for directing clients to Everett only if accounts receive best price and execution AND practice is disclosed to the accounts; because Everett does not disclose the directed trading, Everett violated Standard III(A).

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment: 'Everett may direct new client trades exclusively through Scott Company as long as Everett receives best price and execution on the trades or receives a written statement from new clients that she is not to seek best price and execution and that they are aware of the consequence for their accounts. Everett may trade other accounts through Scott as a reward for directing clients to Everett only if the accounts receive best price and execution and the practice is disclosed to the accounts. Because Everett does not disclose the directed trading, Everett violated Standard III(A).'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Routing OTHER clients' trades to Scott without their knowledge — to win referrals — breaches loyalty and fair dealing; relationship-building is no defense.
rationales.C: Incorrect. A client may direct brokerage; the violation is the undisclosed diversion of other clients' trades.

questions.stem: *Example 4 Rome/Gray*: trust officer uses Gray almost exclusively for trust account brokerage; Gray gives Rome favorable personal prices (lower on buys, higher on sells) than trust accounts. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because Rome's personal account pricing is separate from her professional duties.
options.B: Rome is violating her duty of loyalty to the bank's trust accounts by using Gray for brokerage transactions simply because Gray trades Rome's personal account on favorable terms.
options.C: Rome's only violation is failing to disclose her personal account to her employer.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Selecting Gray for TRUST business because of favorable personal pricing puts self-interest ahead of the accounts she owes loyalty to.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Rome is violating her duty of loyalty to the bank's trust accounts by using Gray for brokerage transactions simply because Gray trades Rome's personal account on favorable terms; Rome is placing her own interests before those of her clients.

Correct. Per Example 4 Comment: 'Rome is violating her duty of loyalty to the bank's trust accounts by using Gray for brokerage transactions simply because Gray trades Rome's personal account on favorable terms. Rome is placing her own interests before those of her clients.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Disclosure would not cure it — the broker-selection decision itself breaches the duty of loyalty to the trust accounts.

questions.stem: *Example 5 Parker/SouthAM*: directs Provo trading desk to give commission business to SouthAM so she can take the trip (without informing supervisor); uses commission dollars to pay for 5-day vacation extension in Rio. *Most likely*:

options.A: no violation — research trip.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: Parker is violating Standard III(A) by not exercising her duty of loyalty to her clients.

rationales.A: Vacation misuse of client brokerage.
rationales.B: Personal vacation.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Parker is violating Standard III(A) by not exercising her duty of loyalty to her clients; must determine whether commissions charged by SouthAM are reasonable in relation to benefit of research provided; must determine whether best execution and prices could be received from SouthAM; the 5 extra days are NOT part of research effort — they do not assist in investment decision-making, so the hotel expenses for the 5 days must NOT be paid for with client commission dollars.

Correct. Per Example 5 Comment: 'Parker is violating Standard III(A) by not exercising her duty of loyalty to her clients. She must determine whether the commissions charged by SouthAM are reasonable in relation to the benefit of the research provided by the trip. She also must determine whether best execution and prices could be received from SouthAM. In addition, the five extra days are not part of the research effort, because they do not assist in the investment decision making. Thus, the hotel expenses for the five days must not be paid for with client commission dollars.'

questions.stem: *Example 6 Knauss/excessive trading*: portfolio manager for HNW individuals; major part of investment management fee based on trading commissions; engages in extensive trading to attain minimum commission level set by her firm; securities are appropriate but volume exceeds what is necessary. *Most likely*:

options.A: Knauss violated Standard III(A) because she is using the assets of her clients to benefit her firm and herself.
options.B: no violation — securities appropriate.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Knauss violated Standard III(A) because she is using the assets of her clients to benefit her firm and herself; even though individual securities are appropriate, excessive turnover to hit firm's commission target places firm/self interests ahead of clients.

Correct. Per Example 6 Comment: 'Knauss violated Standard III(A) because she is using the assets of her clients to benefit her firm and herself.'
rationales.B: Churning = self/firm benefit over clients.
rationales.C: Excessive trading violates.

questions.stem: *Example 7 Dill (managing family accounts)*: father and brother open new fee-paying accounts; IPO comes to market suitable for many of his clients including his brother; allocation short, so to avoid appearance of conflict Dill excludes his brother's account entirely. *Most likely*:

options.A: no violation — avoided conflict.
options.B: Dill violated Standard III(A) because he did not act for the benefit of his brother's account or his other accounts.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Fee-paying family = client.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Dill violated Standard III(A) because he did not act for the benefit of his brother's account or his other accounts; the brother's account is a regular fee-paying account comparable to accounts of his other clients; by not allocating shares proportionately across all accounts for which he thought IPO was suitable, Dill disadvantaged specific clients; would have been correct in not allocating shares to his brother's account IF that account was being managed OUTSIDE the normal fee structure of the firm.

Correct. Per Example 7 Comment: 'Dill violated Standard III(A) because he did not act for the benefit of his brother's account or his other accounts. The brother's account is a regular fee-paying account comparable to the accounts of his other clients. By not allocating the shares proportionately across all accounts for which he thought the IPO was suitable, Dill disadvantaged specific clients. Dill would have been correct in not allocating shares to his brother's account if that account was being managed outside the normal fee structure of the firm.'
rationales.C: Regular client = must include.

questions.stem: *Example 8 Hensley*: expert witness hired by law firm; concerned her testimony may have negative consequences for law firm. *Most likely*:

options.A: As a hired expert, Hensley owes the law firm the same loyalty she would owe an advisory client.
options.B: Hensley should soften her testimony to avoid harming the party that engaged her.
options.C: when acting as expert witness, Hensley is bound by standard of INDEPENDENCE AND OBJECTIVITY in the same manner that an independent research analyst would be bound.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Expert witnesses are bound by independence and objectivity — engagement does not create client-style loyalty over the testimony's content.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Shading testimony for the engaging party is precisely what the independence requirement forbids.
rationales.C:
Full statement: when acting as expert witness, Hensley is bound by standard of INDEPENDENCE AND OBJECTIVITY in the same manner that an independent research analyst would be bound; Hensley must NOT let law firm influence testimony she provides in the legal proceedings; in this situation, the law firm represents Hensley's employer and 'who is the client' is not well defined.

Correct. Per Example 8 Comment: 'In this situation, the law firm represents Hensley's employer and the aspect of "who is the client" is not well defined. When acting as an expert witness, Hensley is bound by the standard of independence and objectivity in the same manner that an independent research analyst would be bound. Hensley must not let the law firm influence the testimony she provides in the legal proceedings.'

questions.stem: *Example 9 Miller/Spears*: Miller manages global financial services mutual fund; Spears (private wealth manager) says her client is investor in Miller's fund and 'we now share responsibility to this client.' *Most likely*:

options.A: Spears' statement is not entirely accurate.
options.B: shared duty.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Spears' statement is not entirely accurate; because SHE provides the advisory services to her new client, she ALONE is bound by the duty of loyalty to this client; Miller's responsibility is to manage the fund according to the investment policy statement of the fund; his actions must NOT be influenced by needs of any particular fund investor.

Correct. Per Example 9 Comment: 'Spears' statement is not entirely accurate. Because she provides the advisory services to her new client, she alone is bound by the duty of loyalty to this client. Miller's responsibility is to manage the fund according to the investment policy statement of the fund. His actions must not be influenced by the needs of any particular fund investor.'
rationales.B: Fund manager loyalty to mandate.
rationales.C: Fund manager to mandate.

questions.stem: *Example 10 Nguyen*: performance analyst notices reporting system missed trade; correcting omission results in large loss for client on 'watch' list; informs appropriate individuals that report needs to be updated before releasing. *Most likely*:

options.A: should not correct.
options.B: Nguyen's actions align with requirements of Standard III(A).
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Correct even if firm loses account.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Nguyen's actions align with requirements of Standard III(A); even though correction may lead to firm's termination by client, withholding information on errors is NOT in best interest of client; loyalty requires accuracy even when unfavorable to member's firm.

Correct. Per Example 10 Comment: 'Nguyen's actions align with the requirements of Standard III(A). Even though the correction may lead to the firm's termination by the client, withholding information on errors is not in the best interest of the client.'
rationales.C: Truth over retention.

questions.stem: *Example 11 Sulejman (execution-only)*: broker who executes only client-directed trades; provides no investment advice. Fiduciary duty imposed by Code and Standards? *Most likely*:

options.A: yes — full fiduciary.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: in this instance, Sulejman serves in an execution-only capacity.

rationales.A: Best execution — no fiduciary.
rationales.B: Execution-only ≠ fiduciary.
rationales.C:
Full statement: in this instance, Sulejman serves in an execution-only capacity; his duty of loyalty, prudence, and care is centered on the skill and diligence used when executing trades — namely by seeking best execution and making trades within the parameters set by the clients (instructions on quantity, price, timing, etc.); acting in the best interests of the client dictates that trades are executed on the most favorable terms that can be achieved for the client; given this job function, the requirements of the Code and Standards for loyalty, prudence, and care clearly do NOT impose a fiduciary duty.

Correct. Per Example 11 Comment: 'In this instance, Sulejman serves in an execution-only capacity. His duty of loyalty, prudence, and care is centered on the skill and diligence used when executing trades — namely, by seeking best execution and making trades within the parameters set by the clients (instructions on quantity, price, timing, etc.). Acting in the best interests of the client dictates that trades are executed on the most favorable terms that can be achieved for the client. Given this job function, the requirements of the Code and Standards for loyalty, prudence, and care clearly do not impose a fiduciary duty.'

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 6 fair treatment when new recommendation made* *most likely*:

options.A: Minimizing the time between the decision and the dissemination of a recommendation; per Standard III(B) compliance practice — shorten the time frame between decision and dissemination is a specific practice for fair dealing.
options.B: Inform all people in firm in advance.
options.C: Distribute recommendations to institutional clients prior to individual accounts.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Standard III(B) Compliance Practices: 'Shorten the time frame between decision and dissemination: Members and candidates should make reasonable efforts to limit the amount of time that elapses between the decision to make an investment recommendation and the time the actual recommendation is disseminated.' Also: 'Limit the number of people involved: Members and candidates should make reasonable efforts to limit the number of people who are aware that a recommendation is going to be disseminated.' — telling everyone in the firm in advance would violate this practice.
rationales.B: Larger group aware could leak.
rationales.C: Prioritizing institutional over individual violates fair dealing.

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 7 Smith/Green Company (buy order after sell change)*: mailed Wednesday, client calls Thursday with buy order. Smith should *most likely*:

options.A: Accept the order.
options.B: advise customer of the change in recommendation before accepting the order.
options.C: Not accept the order because contrary to firm's recommendation.

rationales.A: Client entitled to know.
rationales.B:
Full statement: advise customer of the change in recommendation before accepting the order; per Standard III(B) Guidance: 'Clients who do not know that the member or candidate has changed a recommendation and who, therefore, place orders contrary to a current recommendation should be advised of the changed recommendation before the order is accepted.'

Correct. Per Standard III(B) Guidance: 'Clients who do not know that the member or candidate has changed a recommendation and who, therefore, place orders contrary to a current recommendation should be advised of the changed recommendation before the order is accepted.' The client can still choose to place the order after being informed.
rationales.C: Client's choice.

questions.stem: *Example 2 Rivers/Jackson (fair dealing between funds)*: bank commingled fund gets first crack at new securities and hot issues; discretionary pension accounts second; nondiscretionary accounts lowest priority. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because commingled funds may contractually receive allocation priority over separate accounts.
options.B: Only the nondiscretionary accounts were treated unfairly; the discretionary tier was permissible.
options.C: bank's policy does not treat all customers fairly, and Jackson violated her duty to her clients by giving priority to growth-oriented commingled fund over all other funds and to discretionary accounts over nondiscretionary accounts.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Systematically giving one client group first access to hot issues does not treat all customers fairly under III(B).
rationales.B: Incorrect. The tiered priority disadvantages every group behind the favored fund; the policy as a whole is the violation.
rationales.C:
Full statement: bank's policy does not treat all customers fairly, and Jackson violated her duty to her clients by giving priority to growth-oriented commingled fund over all other funds and to discretionary accounts over nondiscretionary accounts; Jackson must execute orders on a systematic basis to be fair to all clients.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment: 'The bank's policy does not treat all customers fairly, and Jackson violated her duty to her clients by giving priority to the growth-oriented commingled fund over all other funds and to discretionary accounts over nondiscretionary accounts. Jackson must execute orders on a systematic basis to be fair to all clients.'

questions.stem: *Example 3 Morris/PickleDilly (IPO)*: institutional buyers oversubscribe; Morris fills all orders (including his own) but decreases institutional blocks; also has quietly negotiated options for himself. *Most likely*:

options.A: Morris violated Standard III(B) by not treating all customers fairly.
options.B: no violation.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Morris violated Standard III(B) by not treating all customers fairly; to meet obligations, Morris needed to REFRAIN from taking any shares himself and needed to PRORATE the distribution of shares to clients or use some other distribution method for treating clients fairly; should have avoided conflict of interest caused by the options by not seeking those additional benefits; because Morris did not avoid the conflict, he must disclose to his firm and to his clients that he received options as part of the deal (Standard VI(A) Disclosure of Conflicts).

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment: 'Morris violated Standard III(B) by not treating all customers fairly. To meet his obligations under the standard, Morris needed to refrain from taking any shares himself and needed to prorate the distribution of the shares to clients or use some other distribution method for treating clients fairly. In addition, he should have avoided the conflict of interest caused by the options by not seeking those additional benefits. Because Morris did not avoid the conflict, he must disclose to his firm and to his clients that he received options as part of the deal.'
rationales.B: No self-allocation; prorate.
rationales.C: IPO fair allocation + options disclose.

questions.stem: *Example 4 Preston (transaction allocation)*: purchases MBSs; fails to allocate trades immediately; later allocates profitable trades to Colby (major client threatening to leave) and losing trades among other accounts. *Most likely*:

options.A: no violation — retained key client.
options.B: Preston violated Standard III(B) by failing to deal fairly with her clients in taking these investment actions.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Pre-allocation or systematic.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Preston violated Standard III(B) by failing to deal fairly with her clients in taking these investment actions; Preston should have allocated trades PRIOR to executing the orders, OR she should have had a systematic approach to allocating trades, such as pro rata, as soon as it was practical after they were executed.

Correct. Per Example 4 Comment: 'Preston violated Standard III(B) by failing to deal fairly with her clients in taking these investment actions. Preston should have allocated the trades prior to executing the orders, or she should have had a systematic approach to allocating the trades, such as pro rata, as soon as it was practical after they were executed.'
rationales.C: Pre-allocate.

questions.stem: *Example 5 Roberts/SIWM (selective disclosure)*: repeats $1.16 EPS figure in research report to all Coffey clients but informally tells his LARGE clients he expects earnings to be higher (upside surprise). *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because the written report distributed to all clients was comprehensive and accurate.
options.B: Roberts violated Standard II(A) by conveying material nonpublic information to his large clients.
options.C: by not sharing his opinion regarding potential for significant upside earnings surprise with all clients, Roberts did not treat all clients fairly and violated Standard III(B).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Informally giving LARGE clients his higher earnings expectation denied other clients fair access to his opinion — a III(B) violation despite the accurate report.
rationales.B: Incorrect. His own earnings opinion is not issuer MNPI; the failure is unequal treatment of clients under fair dealing.
rationales.C:
Full statement: by not sharing his opinion regarding potential for significant upside earnings surprise with all clients, Roberts did not treat all clients fairly and violated Standard III(B); differential informal advice to only some clients constitutes selective disclosure that disadvantages other clients.

Correct. Per Example 5 Comment: 'By not sharing his opinion regarding the potential for a significant upside earnings surprise with all clients, Roberts did not treat all clients fairly and violated Standard III(B).'

questions.stem: *Example 6 Weng (additional services)*: uses email to issue new recommendation to ALL clients; then calls three largest institutional clients to discuss in detail (they compensate him for personal outreach). *Most likely*:

options.A: Weng did NOT violate Standard III(B).
options.B: violates fair dealing.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Weng did NOT violate Standard III(B); he widely disseminated the recommendation and information to all his clients PRIOR to discussing it with a select few; Weng's largest clients received additional personal service because they PAY HIGHER FEES; IF Weng had discussed the report with a select group of clients PRIOR to distributing it to all his clients, he would have violated Standard III(B).

Correct. Per Example 6 Comment: 'Weng did not violate Standard III(B). He widely disseminated the recommendation and information to all his clients prior to discussing it with a select few. Weng's largest clients received additional personal service because they pay higher fees. If Weng had discussed the report with a select group of clients prior to distributing it to all his clients, he would have violated Standard III(B).'
rationales.B: Widespread first, personal after.
rationales.C: Order matters.

questions.stem: *Example 7 Hampton/Healthy Pharma (minimum lot)*: 3 clients want $10,000 each, 2 clients want $50,000 each; min lot $5,000; Hampton allocated $55,000 total: 3 accounts get $5,000, 2 accounts get $20,000. *Most likely*:

options.A: Hampton violated Standard III(B) because the allocation departed from a strictly pro-rata distribution.
options.B: Hampton did NOT violate Standard III(B), even though the distribution is not on a completely pro-rata basis, because of the required minimum lot size.
options.C: Hampton violated Standard III(C) because a $5,000 position is unsuitable for clients who requested $10,000.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The required minimum lot size justified the departure; fair dealing does not demand strict pro-rata allocation when lot constraints apply.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Hampton did NOT violate Standard III(B), even though the distribution is not on a completely pro-rata basis, because of the required minimum lot size; with total allocation being significantly below amount requested, Hampton ensured that each client received at least minimum lot size of the issue and that filled allocations were close in [proportion].

Correct. Per Example 7 Comment: 'Hampton did not violate Standard III(B), even though the distribution is not on a completely pro-rata basis, because of the required minimum lot size. With the total allocation being significantly below the amount requested, Hampton ensured that each client received at least the minimum lot size of the issue and that the filled allocations were close in [proportion].'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Receiving a smaller-than-requested allocation raises no suitability issue.

questions.stem: *Example 1 Smith/Robertson-Lanai*: two clients same salary; Robertson (60, aggressive, HNW), Lanai (40, low-vol, children's education); Smith recommends 20% in zero-yield small-cap tech to BOTH. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because clients with the same salary may properly receive the same recommendation.
options.B: The recommendation is unsuitable for both clients because zero-yield small-cap technology is inherently speculative.
options.C: in Robertson's case, investment may be appropriate because of financial circumstances and aggressive position, but this investment is NOT suitable for Lanai.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Salary equality does not equalize circumstances; Lanai's low-volatility objective and education funding needs make the position unsuitable for her.
rationales.B: Incorrect. For Robertson — aggressive, high-net-worth — the investment may well be appropriate; suitability is client-specific.
rationales.C:
Full statement: in Robertson's case, investment may be appropriate because of financial circumstances and aggressive position, but this investment is NOT suitable for Lanai; Smith violated Standard III(C) by applying Robertson's investment strategy to Lanai because the two clients' financial circumstances and objectives differ.

Correct. Per Example 1 Comment: 'In Robertson's case, the investment may be appropriate because of his financial circumstances and aggressive investment position, but this investment is not suitable for Lanai. Smith violated Standard III(C) by applying Robertson's investment strategy to Lanai because the two clients' financial circumstances and objectives differ.'

questions.stem: *Example 2 McDowell/Crosby (entire portfolio)*: suggests covered call options for risk-averse client to enhance income; educates client about all possible outcomes including added tax liability and downside protection loss. *Most likely*:

options.A: when determining suitability of an investment, the primary focus should be characteristics of client's ENTIRE PORTFOLIO, not characteristics of single securities on issue-by-issue basis.
options.B: Covered calls are unsuitable for risk-averse clients under any circumstances.
options.C: Suitability is judged option position by option position, without reference to the rest of the client's holdings.

rationales.A:
Full statement: when determining suitability of an investment, the primary focus should be characteristics of client's ENTIRE PORTFOLIO, not characteristics of single securities on issue-by-issue basis; basic characteristics of entire portfolio will largely determine whether investment recommendations are taking client factors into account; here, McDowell properly considers investment in context of entire portfolio and thoroughly explains investment to client.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment: 'When determining suitability of an investment, the primary focus should be the characteristics of the client's entire portfolio, not the characteristics of single securities on an issue-by-issue basis. The basic characteristics of the entire portfolio will largely determine whether investment recommendations are taking client factors into account. In this case, McDowell properly considers the investment in the context of the entire portfolio and thoroughly explains the investment to the client.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. With client education and portfolio-level analysis, income-enhancing covered calls can suit a risk-averse investor.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The example's lesson is the opposite: the ENTIRE PORTFOLIO, not the single position, is the focus of suitability.

questions.stem: *Example 5 Gubler*: IPS requires highly liquid investments; invests 4% in seed private equity fund with 3-year lockup and laddered exit up to 5+ years. *Most likely*:

options.A: no violation — still under equity limit.
options.B: Gubler violated Standard III(C).
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Liquidity mandate violated.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Gubler violated Standard III(C); new investment locks up part of subsidiary's assets for at least 3 years and up to as many as 5 years or more; IPS requires investments in highly liquid investments and describes accepted asset classes; private equity investments with lockup period would not fall within the mandate; even without lockup, asset class with only occasional and thus implicitly illiquid market may not be suitable for the portfolio.

Correct. Per Example 5 Comment: 'Gubler violated Standard III(C). His new investment locks up part of the subsidiary's assets for at least three years and up to as many as five years or more. The IPS requires investments in highly liquid investments and describes accepted asset classes; private equity investments with a lockup period would not fall within the mandate. Even without a lockup period, an asset class with only an occasional and thus implicitly illiquid market may not be suitable for the portfolio.'
rationales.C: IPS mandate violated.

questions.stem: *Example 6 Snead (bonus system)*: retirement fund PM specializing in long-term investments; firm introduces bonus based on quarterly performance vs. peers; Snead buys high-beta stocks contrary to clients' IPSs. *Most likely*:

options.A: no violation — pursuing better returns.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: Snead violated Standard III(C) by investing clients' assets in high-beta stocks.

rationales.A: Bonus incentive doesn't override IPS.
rationales.B: IPS controls, not comp.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Snead violated Standard III(C) by investing clients' assets in high-beta stocks; these high-risk investments are contrary to long-term risk profile established in clients' IPSs; Snead has changed investment strategy of clients in attempt to reap short-term rewards offered by her firm's new compensation arrangement, NOT in response to changes in clients' IPSs.

Correct. Per Example 6 Comment: 'Snead violated Standard III(C) by investing the clients' assets in high-beta stocks. These high-risk investments are contrary to the long-term risk profile established in the clients' IPSs. Snead has changed the investment strategy of the clients in an attempt to reap short-term rewards offered by her firm's new compensation arrangement, not in response to changes in clients' IPSs.'

questions.stem: *Example 7 Van Cleef/MPG (ESG constraint)*: MPG updated IPS to restrict producers of products that negatively affect consumer health; Van Cleef purchases tobacco stock believing return is his first priority. *Most likely*:

options.A: Van Cleef violated Standard III(C) because he failed to consider constraints and unique circumstances of beneficiaries of pension fund.
options.B: There is no violation because maximizing return is the trustee's overriding fiduciary duty.
options.C: There is no violation because ESG restrictions are aspirational guidance rather than binding constraints.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Van Cleef violated Standard III(C) because he failed to consider constraints and unique circumstances of beneficiaries of pension fund; in this case, a preference for incorporating ESG issues into investment process is clearly mandated; trustees have a duty to ensure fund's assets are invested in accordance with IPS.

Correct. Per Example 7 Comment: 'Van Cleef violated Standard III(C) because he failed to consider the constraints and unique circumstances of the beneficiaries of the pension fund. In this case, a preference for incorporating ESG issues into the investment process is clearly mandated. The trustees have a duty to ensure that the fund's assets are invested in accordance with the IPS. Any trustee who is required to abide by the Code and Standards, such as DeVries, would need to ensure that Van Cleef sells the inappropriate tobacco securities.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The updated IPS restriction is a binding constraint; expected return does not override it.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Once written into the IPS, the health-products restriction binds the manager like any other constraint.

questions.stem: *Example 8 Kim (ESG factors permissible)*: family office IPS focused on long-term capital preservation and downside risk mitigation; considers Park (better financials) vs Dong (higher ESG scores); Kim believes ESG factors improve return/risk profile. Invests in Dong. *Most likely*:

options.A: violated — didn't pick best financials.
options.B: Kim has a responsibility to select investments that are suitable for the IPS objectives.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: ESG factors permitted in analysis.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Kim has a responsibility to select investments that are suitable for the IPS objectives; he is PERMITTED to incorporate criteria beyond financial metrics, including but not limited to ESG issues, into the investment decision-making process; Kim's actions are NOT in conflict with his obligation to make effective suitability determinations.

Correct. Per Example 8 Comment: 'Kim has a responsibility to select investments that are suitable for the IPS objectives. He is permitted to incorporate criteria beyond financial metrics, including but not limited to ESG issues, into the investment decision-making process. Kim's actions are not in conflict with his obligation to make effective suitability determinations.'
rationales.C: ESG permissible criteria.

questions.stem: *Unsolicited unsuitable trade request handling* under III(C) *most likely*:

options.A: The member must execute any unsolicited client order immediately, since the account belongs to the client.
options.B: The member should permanently refuse unsuitable unsolicited orders and close the account if the client insists.
options.C: in cases of unsolicited trade requests that a member or candidate KNOWS are unsuitable for a client, member or candidate should refrain from making the trade UNTIL he or she discusses the concerns with the client.

rationales.A: Incorrect. For a KNOWN-unsuitable request, the member should first discuss the concerns with the client rather than execute immediately.
rationales.B: Incorrect. After discussion, the trade may proceed with the client's acknowledgment (and possibly an updated IPS or unsupervised-trade documentation) — refusal-and-termination is not the prescribed course.
rationales.C:
Full statement: in cases of unsolicited trade requests that a member or candidate KNOWS are unsuitable for a client, member or candidate should refrain from making the trade UNTIL he or she discusses the concerns with the client; discussions and resulting actions may encompass a variety of scenarios depending on how the requested unsuitable investment relates to client's full portfolio; in discussing the trade, member/candidate should focus on educating the investor on how the request deviates from current policy statement; member/candidate should require client to acknowledge the discussion, including that the trade is against the advice of the member/candidate because it is unsuitable for the portfolio.

Correct. Per Standard III(C) Guidance on Addressing Unsolicited Trading Requests: 'In cases of unsolicited trade requests that a member or candidate knows are unsuitable for a client, the member or candidate should refrain from making the trade until he or she discusses the concerns with the client... In discussing the trade, the member or candidate should focus on educating the investor on how the request deviates from the current policy statement. The member or candidate should require the client to acknowledge the discussion, including that the trade is against the advice of the member or candidate because it is unsuitable for the portfolio.'

questions.stem: *Managing to index or mandate* under III(C) *most likely*:

options.A: some members and candidates do not manage money for individuals but are responsible for managing a fund to an index or expected mandate.
options.B: Index and mandate managers must still assess each end-investor's personal circumstances before trading.
options.C: Managing to a mandate exempts the manager from Standard III(C) entirely.

rationales.A:
Full statement: some members and candidates do not manage money for individuals but are responsible for managing a fund to an index or expected mandate; the responsibility of these members and candidates is to invest in a manner consistent with the stated mandate; member/candidate who serves as fund manager for large-cap income fund would not be following fund mandate by investing heavily in small-cap companies or start-ups whose stock is speculative in nature; members and candidates who manage pooled assets to a specific mandate are NOT responsible for determining suitability of the fund as an investment for investors who may be purchasing shares in the fund; responsibility for determining suitability of an investment for clients can be conferred only on members and candidates who have an advisory relationship with clients.

Correct. Per Standard III(C) Guidance on Managing to an Index or Mandate: 'Some members and candidates do not manage money for individuals but are responsible for managing a fund to an index or an expected mandate. The responsibility of these members and candidates is to invest in a manner consistent with the stated mandate... Members and candidates who manage pooled assets to a specific mandate are not responsible for determining the suitability of the fund as an investment for investors who may be purchasing shares in the fund. The responsibility for determining the suitability of an investment for clients can be conferred only on members and candidates who have an advisory relationship with clients.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. For pooled mandates, suitability runs to consistency with the stated mandate; investor-level assessment belongs to those who recommend the fund.
rationales.C: Incorrect. III(C)(2) applies directly: recommendations and actions must be consistent with the stated objectives and constraints of the mandate.

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 8 Brown/Green (new client meeting)*: Brown recommends undervalued stock to new client Green after few minutes getting acquainted. *Most likely* Brown should have:

options.A: Explained the characteristics of the company to Green including industry.
options.B: Determined Green's needs, objectives, and tolerance for risk before making a recommendation of any type of security.
options.C: Explained her qualifications including education, training, and experience.

rationales.A: Not the primary issue.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Practice Problem 8 (from question setup): Per Standard III(C)(1)(a): 'Members and Candidates must make a reasonable inquiry into a client's or prospective client's investment experience, risk and return objectives, and financial constraints PRIOR to making any investment recommendation or taking investment action.' Brown must gather this information before recommending any security.
rationales.C: Not the primary issue.

questions.stem: *IPS update triggers* under III(C) *most likely*:

options.A: The IPS need only be revisited when the client requests changes.
options.B: IPS review is required after executing material recommendations, to document what was done.
options.C: IPS should be reviewed at least ANNUALLY and also PRIOR to material changes to any specific investment recommendations or decisions on behalf of the client.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Review must occur at least annually and is the member's affirmative duty.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Review comes PRIOR to material recommendations or decisions, not afterward as documentation.
rationales.C:
Full statement: IPS should be reviewed at least ANNUALLY and also PRIOR to material changes to any specific investment recommendations or decisions on behalf of the client; effort to determine needs and circumstances of each client is NOT a one-time occurrence; investment recommendations or decisions are usually part of ongoing process that takes into account diversity and changing nature of portfolio and client characteristics; passage of time is bound to produce changes important with respect to investment objectives.

Correct. Per Standard III(C) Guidance on Updating an Investment Policy: 'An IPS should be reviewed at least annually and also prior to material changes to any specific investment recommendations or decisions on behalf of the client. The effort to determine the needs and circumstances of each client is not a one-time occurrence.'

questions.stem: *Example 1 Taylor Trust (25% annual growth)*: brochure states firm 'consistently achieves 25% annual growth' when common trust fund increased 25% for only one year (matching overall market); 5-year average is 5%. *Most likely*:

options.A: Taylor's brochure is in violation of Standard III(D).
options.B: no violation — true statement.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Taylor's brochure is in violation of Standard III(D); Taylor must disclose that 25% growth occurred only in one year and only for firm's common trust fund; general claim of firm performance must take into account performance of ALL categories of accounts; by stating that clients can expect a steady 25% annual compound growth rate, Taylor is misrepresenting one portfolio's single-year actual performance as expected performance.

Correct. Per Example 1 Comment: 'Taylor's brochure is in violation of Standard III(D). Taylor must disclose that the 25% growth occurred only in one year and only for the firm's common trust fund. A general claim of firm performance must take into account the performance of all categories of accounts. By stating that clients can expect a steady 25% annual compound growth rate, Taylor is misrepresenting one portfolio's single-year actual performance as expected performance.'
rationales.B: One-year fund ≠ steady rate.
rationales.C: Cherry-picking.

questions.stem: *Example 2 Judd (GIPS asset weighting)*: firm claims GIPS compliance but returns not asset-weighted. *Most likely*:

options.A: no violation — data accurate.
options.B: Judd violated Standard III(D).
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: False GIPS claim.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Judd violated Standard III(D); when claiming compliance with GIPS standards, firms must meet ALL the requirements, make mandatory disclosures, and meet any other requirements that apply to that firm's specific situation; GIPS standards REQUIRE firms to asset-weight portfolio returns to calculate composite returns; Judd's violation is NOT from any misuse of the data but from publishing a performance report with her firm's FALSE claim of GIPS compliance.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment: 'Judd violated Standard III(D). When claiming compliance with the GIPS standards, firms must meet all the requirements, make mandatory disclosures, and meet any other requirements that apply to that firm's specific situation. The GIPS standards require firms to asset weight portfolio returns to calculate composite returns. Judd's violation is not from any misuse of the data but from publishing a performance report with her firm's false claim of GIPS compliance.'
rationales.C: False claim.

questions.stem: *Example 3 McCoy/Mastermind*: recruited from G&P; advertisement for Mastermind includes G&P equity investment performance without identifying it as earned while at G&P. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because the performance figures are real and were genuinely earned by McCoy.
options.B: McCoy violated Standard V(A) by lacking a reasonable basis for the advertised strategy.
options.C: McCoy violated Standard III(D) by distributing advertisement that contains material misrepresentations about historical performance of Mastermind.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Presenting G&P-era performance as Mastermind's without identifying its source is a material misrepresentation of the new firm's record.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The failing is performance presentation under III(D) — misattributed track record — not research diligence.
rationales.C:
Full statement: McCoy violated Standard III(D) by distributing advertisement that contains material misrepresentations about historical performance of Mastermind; Standard III(D) does NOT prohibit showing past performance of accounts managed at prior firm as part of performance track record AS LONG AS showing that record is accompanied by appropriate disclosures about where the performance took place and person's specific role in achieving that performance; if McCoy chooses to use his past performance from G&P in Mastermind's advertising, he must make full disclosure of the source of the historical performance.

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment: 'McCoy violated Standard III(D) by distributing an advertisement that contains material misrepresentations about the historical performance of Mastermind. Standard III(D) requires that members and candidates make reasonable efforts to ensure that performance information is a fair, accurate, and complete representation of an individual's or firm's performance. As a general matter, this standard does not prohibit showing past performance of accounts managed at a prior firm as part of a performance track record as long as showing that record is accompanied by appropriate disclosures about where the performance took place and the person's specific role in achieving that performance.'

questions.stem: *Example 4 Davis (simulated results)*: developed mutual fund selection product; tests methodology by applying retroactively to 2016-22 data producing simulated performance; advertisement includes 2016-22 results without indicating they were simulated. *Most likely*:

options.A: Davis violated Standard III(D) by failing to clearly identify simulated performance results.
options.B: no violation — model tested.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Davis violated Standard III(D) by failing to clearly identify simulated performance results; Standard III(D) prohibits members and candidates from making any statements that misrepresent the performance achieved by them or their firms and requires them to make every reasonable effort to ensure performance information presented to clients is fair, accurate, and complete; Davis's use of simulated results must be accompanied by full disclosure as to source of performance data, including fact that results from 2016 through 2022 are the result of applying the model retroactively to that time period.

Correct. Per Example 4 Comment: 'Davis violated Standard III(D) by failing to clearly identify simulated performance results... Davis's use of simulated results must be accompanied by full disclosure as to the source of the performance data, including the fact that the results from 2016 through 2022 are the result of applying the model retroactively to that time period.'
rationales.B: Simulated must be disclosed.
rationales.C: Backtest disclosure.

questions.stem: *Example 5 Kilmer (composite manipulation)*: composite consists of only a few 'balanced' accounts, excludes accounts under a certain asset level without disclosing exclusion, includes accounts that do NOT have balanced mandate (because they help results), and manipulates accounts included in composite over time. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because composite construction rules are left entirely to each firm's discretion.
options.B: Kilmer violated Standard III(D) by misrepresenting facts in promotional material sent to prospective clients, distorting his firm's performance record, and failing to include disclosures that would have clarified the presentation.
options.C: Kilmer's only obligation was to label the composite 'unaudited' in the promotional material.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Cherry-picked composition, undisclosed exclusions, and out-of-mandate inclusions distort the record — misrepresentation under III(D).
rationales.B: Correct. Per Example 5 Comment: 'Kilmer violated Standard III(D) by misrepresenting the facts in the promotional material sent to prospective clients, distorting his firm's performance record, and failing to include disclosures that would have clarified the presentation.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. An 'unaudited' label does not cure a constructed, misleading performance record.

questions.stem: *Example 6 Purell (attribution methodology change)*: bottom-up firm; attribution shows added value from asset allocation, stock selection contributed negatively; Purell discovers that calculating attribution by comparing each stock with its INDUSTRY (then rolling to sector) improves the manager's stock selection appearance. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because firms may select any attribution methodology they prefer.
options.B: Purell must restate all historical attribution under the original methodology before publishing new results.
options.C: Purell should include disclosures on any methodology changes so that clients have sufficient information to judge the performance generated by the firm.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Methodology choice is permitted, but CHANGES must be disclosed so clients can judge the performance presented.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Restatement is not the requirement; disclosure of the change and its effect is.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Purell should include disclosures on any methodology changes so that clients have sufficient information to judge the performance generated by the firm; changing methodology solely to make performance look better without proper disclosure is misleading; complete information is provided when clients have sufficient information to judge performance.

Correct. Per Example 6 (partial): 'Complete information is provided when clients have sufficient information to judge the performance generated by the firm.' Selecting methodology to improve appearance without disclosure violates III(D).

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 9 Cupp (new account inclusion)*: firm's policy is to add new accounts after first full month; Cupp notices new account holding acquired, doubling value; Cupp decides to account for new portfolio as of transfer date to include the gain. *Most likely*:

options.A: Cupp violated Code and Standards because inclusion of new account produces an inaccurate calculation of the monthly results according to the firm's stated policies.
options.B: There is no violation because GIPS permits firms to amend composite-inclusion policies when circumstances warrant.
options.C: There is no violation because each company retains discretion to determine when new accounts enter its composites.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Cupp violated Code and Standards because inclusion of new account produces an inaccurate calculation of the monthly results according to the firm's stated policies; deviating from firm's stated policy to opportunistically capture favorable performance is a violation.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 9: Cupp deviated from the firm's stated policy of adding new accounts after the first full month specifically to include a favorable event. This produces an inaccurate calculation of monthly results per the firm's stated policies. Per Standard III(D), performance must be fair, accurate, and complete — including consistent application of stated policies.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Whatever flexibility exists to amend policies prospectively, Cupp applied an exception to capture a favorable result under the EXISTING stated policy.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Discretion is exercised through the firm's stated policy — adding the account early, against that policy, makes the monthly result inaccurate.

questions.stem: *Example 1 Connor (internal reports)*: financial analyst gives investment advice to trustees of City Medical; trustees provide internal reports about physical plant renovation/expansion; local businessman Kasey asks Connor for building plans to decide between institutions. *Most likely*:

options.A: no violation — Kasey is potential donor.
options.B: trustees gave Connor internal reports so she could advise them on how to manage their endowment funds.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Prohibited disclosure.
rationales.B:
Full statement: trustees gave Connor internal reports so she could advise them on how to manage their endowment funds; because information in reports is clearly both confidential AND within scope of the confidential relationship, Standard III(E) prohibits Connor from divulging the information to Kasey.

Correct. Per Example 1 Comment: 'The trustees gave Connor the internal reports so she could advise them on how to manage their endowment funds. Because the information in the reports is clearly both confidential and within the scope of the confidential relationship, Standard III(E) prohibits Connor from divulging the information to Kasey.'
rationales.C: Confidential info scope.

questions.stem: *Example 2 Moody (charity solicitation)*: advisory client mentioned $50,000 charitable donation to reduce taxes; Moody (treasurer of Home for Indigent Widows) recommends HIW VP call on her client to ask for donation. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because Moody acted with charitable intent and expected no personal benefit.
options.B: There is no violation because the client's donation plans were not investment information.
options.C: even though attempt to help Home for Indigent Widows was well-intended, Moody violated Standard III(E) by revealing confidential information about her client.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Good intentions do not excuse revealing confidential client information; III(E) was violated.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Confidentiality covers information about the client learned in the professional relationship, financial plans included.
rationales.C:
Full statement: even though attempt to help Home for Indigent Widows was well-intended, Moody violated Standard III(E) by revealing confidential information about her client; well-intentioned motives do not excuse confidentiality breach.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment: 'Even though the attempt to help the Home for Indigent Widows was well intended, Moody violated Standard III(E) by revealing confidential information about her client.'

questions.stem: *Example 3 Samuel/Garcia (possible illegal activity)*: PM for Garcia's pension plan; learns of potentially excessive/improper CEO charges to pension plan; government officials contact Samuel with request to examine records. *Most likely*:

options.A: Samuel and her employer should seek advice of legal counsel to determine appropriate steps to take to protect interests of participants and beneficiaries of pension plan and comply with applicable law for responding to government regulators.
options.B: Samuel must refuse all cooperation, since Standard III(E) makes client confidentiality absolute.
options.C: Samuel should immediately deliver the pension records to the officials without any consultation.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Samuel and her employer should seek advice of legal counsel to determine appropriate steps to take to protect interests of participants and beneficiaries of pension plan and comply with applicable law for responding to government regulators; Samuel may well have a duty to provide pension fund records her firm possesses to the government.

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment: 'Samuel and her employer should seek the advice of legal counsel to determine the appropriate steps to take to protect the interests of the participants and beneficiaries of the pension plan and comply with applicable law for responding to government regulators. Samuel may well have a duty to provide the pension fund records her firm possesses to the government.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Confidentiality yields where illegal activity is suspected and law may require disclosure; the response is legal-counsel guidance, not blanket refusal.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The prescribed step is seeking legal counsel's advice to protect the plan's participants — not unadvised unilateral disclosure.

questions.stem: *Example 4 Bradford (embezzlement suspicion)*: manages real estate development corp money + personal portfolios of officers including CFO; based on records and questionable practices believes CFO is embezzling. *Most likely*:

options.A: Bradford must immediately publicize his embezzlement suspicions to protect the market.
options.B: Bradford should check with his firm's compliance department or appropriate legal counsel to determine whether applicable securities regulations require reporting the CFO's financial records to authorities.
options.C: Bradford owes the CFO personal confidentiality and should take no action at all.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Going public first is not the course; checking with compliance or legal counsel about reporting obligations is.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Example 4 Comment: 'Bradford should check with his firm's compliance department or appropriate legal counsel to determine whether applicable securities regulations require reporting the CFO's financial records to authorities.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Suspected illegal activity is an exception to confidentiality; inaction is not an option.

questions.stem: *Example 5 Moody (accidental disclosure via social media)*: sets up LTC group page with instructions warning against posting confidential info; client posts personal details before Moody removes them. *Most likely*:

options.A: Moody violates.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: Moody has taken reasonable steps to protect confidentiality of client information while using social media platform.

rationales.A: Reasonable precautions taken.
rationales.B: Not a violation.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Moody has taken reasonable steps to protect confidentiality of client information while using social media platform; provided instructions clarifying that all information posted on the site would be publicly viewable to all group members and warned against using this method for communicating confidential information; accidental disclosure of confidential information by a client is not under Moody's control; her actions to remove the information promptly once she became aware further align with Standard III(E).

Correct. Per Example 5 Comment: 'Moody has taken reasonable steps to protect the confidentiality of client information while using the social media platform. She provided instructions clarifying that all information posted on the site would be publicly viewable to all group members and warned against using this method for communicating confidential information. The accidental disclosure of confidential information by a client is not under Moody's control. Her actions to remove the information promptly once she became aware further align with Standard III(E).'

questions.stem: *Example 6 Gonzales/Brennan (vulnerable investor)*: onboarded Brennan with son as secondary contact; Gonzales notices memory lapses; Brennan directs 50% portfolio liquidation for speculative venture; Gonzales contacts son before acting. *Most likely*:

options.A: vulnerable investors — Standard III(A) requires diligently working to safeguard interests of all clients including potentially vulnerable investors.
options.B: Contacting the son violated Standard III(E), since Brennan never consented to disclosure of the trade request.
options.C: Gonzales must execute the liquidation immediately because the client's instructions are controlling.

rationales.A:
Full statement: vulnerable investors — Standard III(A) requires diligently working to safeguard interests of all clients including potentially vulnerable investors; when member has established secondary contact at beginning of client relationship (trusted family member/legal adviser), that contact provides an avenue to prevent/address potential financial abuse; documentation of conversations and reasons for disclosing sensitive information should be retained in client files; Gonzales's proactive contact of designated secondary contact aligns with pre-established parameters and does not violate III(E).

Correct. Per Standard III(E) Guidance on Vulnerable Investors and Example 6: When a secondary contact was established at the beginning of the relationship (Brennan's son), Gonzales can properly reach out when concerns arise about ability to make informed decisions. Documentation of the conversations aligns with III(E) requirements.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The son was established as SECONDARY CONTACT at onboarding — activating that arrangement protects a potentially vulnerable client rather than breaching confidentiality.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Safeguarding a vulnerable investor under III(A) supports pausing and engaging the designated contact before executing a drastic, uncharacteristic order.

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 11 Bronson/Murdock (alumni request)*: Bronson advises university endowment; prominent alumnus Murdock calls Friday afternoon claiming urgent need for comprehensive financial info to close potential contributor deal; cannot contact trustees. *Most likely* Bronson should:

options.A: Send Murdock the information because disclosure benefits client.
options.B: Not send Murdock the information to preserve confidentiality; only trustees can authorize disclosure of the confidential fund information — Murdock is a third party.
options.C: Send it, provided Bronson promptly notifies the trustees.

rationales.A: Third party requires client authorization.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard III(E) and Practice Problem 11: Confidential client information cannot be disclosed to third parties without client consent (client = the endowment trustees). Even the client's ultimate benefit does not override the duty to preserve confidentiality without proper authorization from those with authority to consent — the trustees. Notifying after the fact does not cure the violation.
rationales.C: Post-hoc notice insufficient.

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 12 Townsend (charity fundraising)*: Townsend appointed to board of local youth golf program (chapter of national not-for-profit); includes small section in regular client newsletter discussing fundraising campaign and her position on board. *Most likely*:

options.A: Violated by soliciting.
options.B: Violated by not getting approval.
options.C: Townsend did not violate the Code and Standards.

rationales.A: General newsletter is not targeting.
rationales.B: No approval requirement.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Townsend did not violate the Code and Standards; using her professional communications to inform clients about a charitable opportunity while disclosing her board role is a legitimate outreach, not a solicitation targeting specific clients based on confidential client information; there is no misuse of confidential financial details.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 12: No confidential client information is used, no client is targeted based on private info, and inclusion in a general newsletter is transparent (with disclosure of her board role). This does not violate the Code and Standards.

drills.los_text: recommend practices and procedures designed to prevent violations of the Code and Standards
drills.reading_name: Guidance for Standard III: Duties to Clients

questions.stem: *Regular account information* practice under III(A) *most likely*:

options.A: members and candidates with control of client assets should (1) submit to each client, at least QUARTERLY, an itemized statement showing funds and securities in custody or possession of member/candidate plus all debits, credits, and transactions that occurred during the period.
options.B: An annual summary statement satisfies the account-information practice for clients whose assets the member controls.
options.C: Account statements are the custodian's responsibility, so the member need send nothing.

rationales.A:
Full statement: members and candidates with control of client assets should (1) submit to each client, at least QUARTERLY, an itemized statement showing funds and securities in custody or possession of member/candidate plus all debits, credits, and transactions that occurred during the period; (2) DISCLOSE to client where assets are maintained, as well as where or when they are moved; and (3) SEPARATE client's assets from any other party's assets, including member's/candidate's own assets.

Correct. Per Standard III(A) Compliance Practices: 'Regular account information: Members and candidates with control of client assets should (1) submit to each client, at least quarterly, an itemized statement showing the funds and securities in the custody or possession of the member or candidate plus all debits, credits, and transactions that occurred during the period, (2) disclose to the client where the assets are maintained, as well as where or when they are moved, and (3) separate the client's assets from any other party's assets, including the member's or candidate's own assets.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The practice is an itemized statement at least QUARTERLY, showing custody funds/securities and period activity.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Members with control of client assets carry the reporting practice themselves.

questions.stem: *Client approval when uncertain* under III(A) *most likely*:

options.A: When uncertain about the right course, the member should proceed unilaterally on professional judgment alone.
options.B: if member/candidate is uncertain about appropriate course of action with respect to client, member/candidate should consider what he or she would expect or demand if they were the client.
options.C: Uncertainty should be resolved by following whatever maximizes the account's short-term return.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The guidance is to consider what one would expect or demand as the client — and, in practice, to disclose and seek approval.
rationales.B:
Full statement: if member/candidate is uncertain about appropriate course of action with respect to client, member/candidate should consider what he or she would expect or demand if they were the client; if in doubt, member/candidate should disclose the questionable matter in WRITING to the client and OBTAIN CLIENT APPROVAL.

Correct. Per Standard III(A) Compliance Practices: 'Client approval: If a member or candidate is uncertain about the appropriate course of action with respect to a client, the member or candidate should consider what he or she would expect or demand if the member or candidate were the client. If in doubt, a member or candidate should disclose the questionable matter in writing to the client and obtain client approval.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The touchstone is the client's perspective and interests, not short-term return.

questions.stem: *Diversification* under III(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Diversification is optional under III(A) whenever the manager holds high conviction in a position.
options.B: Managers must diversify even when plan guidelines or account terms direct concentration.
options.C: members and candidates should DIVERSIFY investments to reduce the risk of loss, UNLESS diversification is not consistent with plan guidelines or is contrary to the account objectives.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Diversification to reduce loss risk is the default duty; conviction does not suspend it.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The duty yields where diversification is inconsistent with plan guidelines or contrary to the account's terms.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Standard III(A) Compliance Practices: 'Diversify: Members and candidates should diversify investments to reduce the risk of loss, unless diversification is not consistent with plan guidelines or is contrary to the account objectives.'

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 3 Carter (new low-fee trading scheme)*: broker offers new low-fee execution-only trades; commissions on new service applied toward soft dollar commitment. *Most likely*:

options.A: Carter can trade for his accounts that prohibit soft dollar arrangements under the new, low-fee trading scheme.
options.B: Carter cannot use new scheme.
options.C: Carter should increase trading volume.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Carter can trade for his accounts that prohibit soft dollar arrangements under the new, low-fee trading scheme; the new low-fee trades are comparable to fees of other brokers currently used, so they do not carry the elevated soft-dollar commission that would violate those accounts' restrictions.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 3: The new low-fee trades are execution-only and comparable to fees other brokers charge, so using them for accounts that prohibit soft dollar arrangements does not involve the client paying soft-dollar surcharges. The prohibition on soft dollar arrangements is not violated when trades are being executed at competitive market rates.
rationales.B: Low-fee execution OK for no-soft-dollar accounts.
rationales.C: Should not artificially increase volume.

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 4 manager's use of client brokerage commissions violation* *most likely*:

options.A: Client may direct manager to use brokerage commissions for that client.
options.B: Client brokerage commissions may be directed to pay for the investment manager's operating expenses.
options.C: Should benefit client and be commensurate with services received.

rationales.A: Directed brokerage OK.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Client brokerage commissions may be directed to pay for the investment manager's operating expenses; this violates the duty of loyalty because brokerage commissions are an asset of the CLIENT, not the manager, and using them for manager's operating expenses benefits the manager, not the client.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 4: Client brokerage commissions must be used to benefit the client. Using them to pay the investment manager's operating expenses would mean the manager is using client assets to pay for their own overhead — a violation of the duty of loyalty. Per Standard III(A) Guidance on Soft Commission Policies: 'A member or candidate who pays a higher brokerage commission than he or she would normally pay to allow for the purchase of goods or services, without corresponding benefit to the client, violates the duty of loyalty to the client.'
rationales.C: Client benefit is proper use.

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 5 Stafford (soft dollars for CFA fees)*: uses fund's soft dollar account to pay CFA Program registration and exam fees. *Most likely*:

options.A: No violation — prospectus disclosed policies.
options.B: CFA Program doesn't meet research definition.
options.C: Stafford violated the Code and Standards because improving her investment skills is not a reasonable use of the soft dollar account.

rationales.A: Personal skill development ≠ research for client.
rationales.B: Primary issue is benefit direction.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Stafford violated the Code and Standards because improving her investment skills is not a reasonable use of the soft dollar account; while soft dollars can be used for research services that benefit the client, using them for personal certification fees benefits Stafford personally and represents an expense that should be borne by her or her employer, not fund shareholders.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 5: Personal certification fees are a professional/career benefit to the individual, not a research service benefiting the client. Even with prospectus disclosure of soft-dollar policies, using client commissions to pay for personal development is a violation of duty of loyalty. The primary issue is the appropriateness of the use, not whether the CFA Program constitutes research.

questions.stem: *Directed brokerage disclosure* under III(A) *most likely*:

options.A: when clients direct manager to use specific brokerage firm for trades (directed brokerage), because brokerage commission is an asset of the client and is used to benefit that client, not the manager, this practice does not violate duty of loyalty.
options.B: Directed brokerage requires no disclosure because the client, not the manager, chose the broker.
options.C: Members should refuse all directed-brokerage arrangements as inherent best-execution violations.

rationales.A:
Full statement: when clients direct manager to use specific brokerage firm for trades (directed brokerage), because brokerage commission is an asset of the client and is used to benefit that client, not the manager, this practice does not violate duty of loyalty; the member or candidate should, however, seek CONFIRMATION from the client that goods or services purchased from the brokerage will benefit the account beneficiaries; in addition, member or candidate should DISCLOSE to the client that the client may not be getting best execution from the directed brokerage.

Correct. Per Standard III(A) Guidance on Soft Commission Policies: 'from time to time, some clients will direct a manager to use a specific brokerage firm for all or some of the client's trades... The member or candidate should, however, seek confirmation from the client that the goods or services purchased from the brokerage will benefit the account beneficiaries. In addition, the member or candidate should disclose to the client that the client may not be getting best execution from the directed brokerage.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Because commissions are the client's asset, the member should disclose that the direction may cost the client best execution.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Directed brokerage is permissible; the duty is disclosure of the potential best-execution consequence.

questions.stem: *Disseminate based on indications of interest and suitability* under III(B) *most likely*:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: make initial investment recommendations available to all clients who indicate an interest.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Interest + suitability + simultaneous.
rationales.B:
